Storms produced significant wind damage along with a few brief tornadoes as they pushed southeast through the DFW Metroplex, with additional severe weather reports continuing as storms pushed southeast into Central and East Texas. Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Event Narrative: Cars were stalled in water at Sam Reynolds Rd and Boss Range Road in Justin. The road and the bridge were shut down. The wood frame of a house under construction was completely knocked down in thunderstorm winds in the Bar C Ranch neighborhood near Saginaw. An area of enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and a few homes in North Richland Hills stretching from Dewsbury Street to Richfield Park. The wind speeds are consistent with winds around 70-75 mph. Two homes sustained significant damage when the strong winds pushed the garage doors in, causing damage to the rest of the house. Other scattered tree damage was noted in the remainder of the neighborhood. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along a west to east line extending down parts of Shady Hill Lane, across Green Oaks Blvd, and down Ivy Hill and Marshfield Ct. Many large tree limbs were broken off over halfway up on the tree trunks, and some trees were uprooted. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes. Broadcast media reported quarter size hail in the city of Bedford, TX. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There were several large tree limbs broken and one tree was snapped. One building did have some window damage as well. This damage occurred around Rolling Hills Country|Club, just north of Interstate 30. A public report (including photos) indicated roof damage and large tree branches blown down in the northwest part of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire Department reported a small fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco. The fire was caused by lightning and resulted in approximately 1,000 dollars in damage. Many large trees sustained significant damage on the property of the North Central Texas Council of Governments. Some trees were cracked at the base and very large branches were broken off at least a dozen trees. A few trees were snapped partially up the trunk. Most of the trees were about 3-5 feet in diameter. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a brief tornado occurred in Arlington across a neighborhood east of Collins Street. The pattern of the damage in this neighborhood indicated a brief EF-1 tornado that dissipated before reaching the AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Park area. A few homes in the neighborhood had significant loss of roofing and several trees were snapped. Additional damage in Arlington was a result of straight-line winds of 60-70 mph. Several trees and homes suffered damage along and around Central Ave in Newark. Video obtained from the local fire station indicates the cause was straight-line winds. Many trees were snapped, large tree limb broken, and a few large trees were uprooted. Some of the trees damaged homes and vehicles. Some roofs sustained shingle damage. Water was over the road at Ben-Bert Rd and Hwy 114 with a depth of 1.5 feet. An area of enhanced wind damage occurred from near Hwy 121 and Bedford Rd in Bedford to just north of Hwy 183 on Main Street in Euless. Scattered significant tree damage was observed along this path, walkway awnings were damaged at Trinity High School, and the roof was damaged at the Napa Auto shop on Main Street. All the damage was pushed to the east and maximum winds were estimated at 65-70 mph. Medium sized tree branches were snapped off in the Tehama Ridge Subdivision in north Fort Worth. Part of the metal roof was torn off an election building on Premier Street in Fort Worth. Emergency management reported that a 69 MPH wind gust was measured in the city of Dalworthington Gardens, TX. Strong thunderstorm winds moved across the City of Irving causing scattered damage across the city. Most of the damage was to trees. Damage began at the Promenade at Valley Ridge apartments carport awnings were damaged. The next most significant damage was to the roof of an apartment building at Stonehill Terrace. Part of the roof was lifted off of one building. Next, a vacant building sustained significant damage near Walnut Hill and Hurd Dr. The facade was ripped off one corner, leaving the inside of the building exposed. The damage ended at the Mustang Rec Center where the roof was damaged. The damage pattern through the city suggests several small downbursts caused the most significant damage with winds of mostly 65-70 mph but maximum winds at times may have reached near 80 mph. Amateur radio reported nickel size hail in the Pelican Bay area of Eagle Mountain Lake. A National Weather Service employee reported a wind gust of 59 MPH in the north part of the city of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire and Rescue reported a house fire started by a lightning strike at the 7175 block of Maple Street. The strike caused an electrical surge which ignited the gas feed coming into the stove, creating 5,000 dollars in damage. A video showed a brief tornado over Eagle Mountain Lake along the leading edge of the gust front. No damage was reported from this tornado. A National Weather Service damage survey found an EF-0 tornado with maximum winds of 85 MPH on the far north side of Fort Worth. The tornado began on the west end of Aldersyde Drive in the Crawford Farms Subdivision, tracked east for about one and one-quarter mile, lifting just east of the Vista Meadows Subdivision. Damage to roofs, windows, and siding occurred on many single family homes, as well as tree limbs blown off of mostly Bradford pear trees. The most significant damage was found on Sourwood Drive where one home had more than 20% of the roof decking removed,|windows broken, and a garage door blown in. This damage was consistent with a high-end EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 mph. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There was minor roof damage seen across this area and large tree limbs broken. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes and an apartment complex. This damage occurred southwest of AT&T Stadium between|Cooper St and AT&T Way. A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base Automated Weather Observation System (AWOS). Large tree limbs were knocked down in the Village of Woodland Springs in North Fort Worth/Keller. Large trees were snapped and the roof of an apartment building suffered minor damage from strong thunderstorms winds. The report was near Silver Creek Rd and Bomber Rd in White Settlement. The nearby AWOS reported a 63 MPH wind gust. Public reports indicated numerous trees down and homes/roofs damaged in Haltom City. The Fort Worth Alliance Airport Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) reported a wind gust of 66 MPH. Large tree limbs were broken off on NW 28th Street near Meacham Blvd in Fort Worth. A manufactured home on Private Road 4866 in Briar was damaged and shifted back about 4 feet by thunderstorms winds. The skirting of the home was peeled and windows were knocked out or blown out. In addition, a large metal garage system was damaged. The garage doors were pushed in and part of the metal roof was peeled off. Another public report indicated additional home and tree damage in the Briar area, particularly in the Hills of Briar Oak Addition. A trained spotter reported approximately a dozen downed tree branches throughout The Canyons Apartment complex, several with broken branch diameters of 4- 6. One entire dogwood tree appeared snapped at the base, approximately 6 or so in diameter. Another main trunk section of crape myrtle approximately 4 diameter appeared twisted from the top of the tree at about a 10'-15' height from the ground and blown over car(s) and dropped about 50 yards away. There were also several downed tree branches across the levee at the former Lockheed Martin Recreational area at the baseball field right field fence. A couple of roof tiles were blown off; this is a newly installed roof (installed approximately 12 months ago). There is some additional notable damage up River Bend Drive from River Park Drive, such as some tree branches and several examples of decorative cement toppers for brick columns facing River Bend having been blown off either completely or just chunks of the toppers blown off. None of the connected brick columns appear to have been damaged.

The events caused property damage valued at 821000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Storms produced significant wind damage along with a few brief tornadoes as they pushed southeast through the DFW Metroplex, with additional severe weather reports continuing as storms pushed southeast into Central and East Texas. Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Event Narrative: Cars were stalled in water at Sam Reynolds Rd and Boss Range Road in Justin. The road and the bridge were shut down. The wood frame of a house under construction was completely knocked down in thunderstorm winds in the Bar C Ranch neighborhood near Saginaw. An area of enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and a few homes in North Richland Hills stretching from Dewsbury Street to Richfield Park. The wind speeds are consistent with winds around 70-75 mph. Two homes sustained significant damage when the strong winds pushed the garage doors in, causing damage to the rest of the house. Other scattered tree damage was noted in the remainder of the neighborhood. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along a west to east line extending down parts of Shady Hill Lane, across Green Oaks Blvd, and down Ivy Hill and Marshfield Ct. Many large tree limbs were broken off over halfway up on the tree trunks, and some trees were uprooted. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes. Broadcast media reported quarter size hail in the city of Bedford, TX. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There were several large tree limbs broken and one tree was snapped. One building did have some window damage as well. This damage occurred around Rolling Hills Country|Club, just north of Interstate 30. A public report (including photos) indicated roof damage and large tree branches blown down in the northwest part of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire Department reported a small fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco. The fire was caused by lightning and resulted in approximately 1,000 dollars in damage. Many large trees sustained significant damage on the property of the North Central Texas Council of Governments. Some trees were cracked at the base and very large branches were broken off at least a dozen trees. A few trees were snapped partially up the trunk. Most of the trees were about 3-5 feet in diameter. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a brief tornado occurred in Arlington across a neighborhood east of Collins Street. The pattern of the damage in this neighborhood indicated a brief EF-1 tornado that dissipated before reaching the AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Park area. A few homes in the neighborhood had significant loss of roofing and several trees were snapped. Additional damage in Arlington was a result of straight-line winds of 60-70 mph. Several trees and homes suffered damage along and around Central Ave in Newark. Video obtained from the local fire station indicates the cause was straight-line winds. Many trees were snapped, large tree limb broken, and a few large trees were uprooted. Some of the trees damaged homes and vehicles. Some roofs sustained shingle damage. Water was over the road at Ben-Bert Rd and Hwy 114 with a depth of 1.5 feet. An area of enhanced wind damage occurred from near Hwy 121 and Bedford Rd in Bedford to just north of Hwy 183 on Main Street in Euless. Scattered significant tree damage was observed along this path, walkway awnings were damaged at Trinity High School, and the roof was damaged at the Napa Auto shop on Main Street. All the damage was pushed to the east and maximum winds were estimated at 65-70 mph. Medium sized tree branches were snapped off in the Tehama Ridge Subdivision in north Fort Worth. Part of the metal roof was torn off an election building on Premier Street in Fort Worth. Emergency management reported that a 69 MPH wind gust was measured in the city of Dalworthington Gardens, TX. Strong thunderstorm winds moved across the City of Irving causing scattered damage across the city. Most of the damage was to trees. Damage began at the Promenade at Valley Ridge apartments carport awnings were damaged. The next most significant damage was to the roof of an apartment building at Stonehill Terrace. Part of the roof was lifted off of one building. Next, a vacant building sustained significant damage near Walnut Hill and Hurd Dr. The facade was ripped off one corner, leaving the inside of the building exposed. The damage ended at the Mustang Rec Center where the roof was damaged. The damage pattern through the city suggests several small downbursts caused the most significant damage with winds of mostly 65-70 mph but maximum winds at times may have reached near 80 mph. Amateur radio reported nickel size hail in the Pelican Bay area of Eagle Mountain Lake. A National Weather Service employee reported a wind gust of 59 MPH in the north part of the city of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire and Rescue reported a house fire started by a lightning strike at the 7175 block of Maple Street. The strike caused an electrical surge which ignited the gas feed coming into the stove, creating 5,000 dollars in damage. A video showed a brief tornado over Eagle Mountain Lake along the leading edge of the gust front. No damage was reported from this tornado. A National Weather Service damage survey found an EF-0 tornado with maximum winds of 85 MPH on the far north side of Fort Worth. The tornado began on the west end of Aldersyde Drive in the Crawford Farms Subdivision, tracked east for about one and one-quarter mile, lifting just east of the Vista Meadows Subdivision. Damage to roofs, windows, and siding occurred on many single family homes, as well as tree limbs blown off of mostly Bradford pear trees. The most significant damage was found on Sourwood Drive where one home had more than 20% of the roof decking removed,|windows broken, and a garage door blown in. This damage was consistent with a high-end EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 mph. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There was minor roof damage seen across this area and large tree limbs broken. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes and an apartment complex. This damage occurred southwest of AT&T Stadium between|Cooper St and AT&T Way. A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base Automated Weather Observation System (AWOS). Large tree limbs were knocked down in the Village of Woodland Springs in North Fort Worth/Keller. Large trees were snapped and the roof of an apartment building suffered minor damage from strong thunderstorms winds. The report was near Silver Creek Rd and Bomber Rd in White Settlement. The nearby AWOS reported a 63 MPH wind gust. Public reports indicated numerous trees down and homes/roofs damaged in Haltom City. The Fort Worth Alliance Airport Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) reported a wind gust of 66 MPH. Large tree limbs were broken off on NW 28th Street near Meacham Blvd in Fort Worth. A manufactured home on Private Road 4866 in Briar was damaged and shifted back about 4 feet by thunderstorms winds. The skirting of the home was peeled and windows were knocked out or blown out. In addition, a large metal garage system was damaged. The garage doors were pushed in and part of the metal roof was peeled off. Another public report indicated additional home and tree damage in the Briar area, particularly in the Hills of Briar Oak Addition. A trained spotter reported approximately a dozen downed tree branches throughout The Canyons Apartment complex, several with broken branch diameters of 4- 6. One entire dogwood tree appeared snapped at the base, approximately 6 or so in diameter. Another main trunk section of crape myrtle approximately 4 diameter appeared twisted from the top of the tree at about a 10'-15' height from the ground and blown over car(s) and dropped about 50 yards away. There were also several downed tree branches across the levee at the former Lockheed Martin Recreational area at the baseball field right field fence. A couple of roof tiles were blown off; this is a newly installed roof (installed approximately 12 months ago). There is some additional notable damage up River Bend Drive from River Park Drive, such as some tree branches and several examples of decorative cement toppers for brick columns facing River Bend having been blown off either completely or just chunks of the toppers blown off. None of the connected brick columns appear to have been damaged.

In addition, a large metal garage system was damaged. The garage doors were pushed in and part of the metal roof was peeled off. Another public report indicated additional home and tree damage in the Briar area, particularly in the Hills of Briar Oak Addition. A trained spotter reported approximately a dozen downed tree branches throughout The Canyons Apartment complex, several with broken branch diameters of 4- 6. One entire dogwood tree appeared snapped at the base, approximately 6 or so in diameter. Another main trunk section of crape myrtle approximately 4 diameter appeared twisted from the top of the tree at about a 10'-15' height from the ground and blown over car(s) and dropped about 50 yards away. There were also several downed tree branches across the levee at the former Lockheed Martin Recreational area at the baseball field right field fence. A couple of roof tiles were blown off; this is a newly installed roof (installed approximately 12 months ago). There is some additional notable damage up River Bend Drive from River Park Drive, such as some tree branches and several examples of decorative cement toppers for brick columns facing River Bend having been blown off either completely or just chunks of the toppers blown off. None of the connected brick columns appear to have been damaged.

The following events were reported: Flood, Hail, Lightning, Thunderstorm Wind, Tornado. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 28.8603 km away, between 2019-06-16 02:00:00 and 2019-06-16 15:51:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 53.45333333333334 inches. The flood was caused by Heavy Rain. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 821000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Storms produced significant wind damage along with a few brief tornadoes as they pushed southeast through the DFW Metroplex, with additional severe weather reports continuing as storms pushed southeast into Central and East Texas. Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Event Narrative: Cars were stalled in water at Sam Reynolds Rd and Boss Range Road in Justin. The road and the bridge were shut down. The wood frame of a house under construction was completely knocked down in thunderstorm winds in the Bar C Ranch neighborhood near Saginaw. An area of enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and a few homes in North Richland Hills stretching from Dewsbury Street to Richfield Park. The wind speeds are consistent with winds around 70-75 mph. Two homes sustained significant damage when the strong winds pushed the garage doors in, causing damage to the rest of the house. Other scattered tree damage was noted in the remainder of the neighborhood. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along a west to east line extending down parts of Shady Hill Lane, across Green Oaks Blvd, and down Ivy Hill and Marshfield Ct. Many large tree limbs were broken off over halfway up on the tree trunks, and some trees were uprooted. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes. Broadcast media reported quarter size hail in the city of Bedford, TX. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There were several large tree limbs broken and one tree was snapped. One building did have some window damage as well. This damage occurred around Rolling Hills Country|Club, just north of Interstate 30. A public report (including photos) indicated roof damage and large tree branches blown down in the northwest part of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire Department reported a small fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco. The fire was caused by lightning and resulted in approximately 1,000 dollars in damage. Many large trees sustained significant damage on the property of the North Central Texas Council of Governments. Some trees were cracked at the base and very large branches were broken off at least a dozen trees. A few trees were snapped partially up the trunk. Most of the trees were about 3-5 feet in diameter. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a brief tornado occurred in Arlington across a neighborhood east of Collins Street. The pattern of the damage in this neighborhood indicated a brief EF-1 tornado that dissipated before reaching the AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Park area. A few homes in the neighborhood had significant loss of roofing and several trees were snapped. Additional damage in Arlington was a result of straight-line winds of 60-70 mph. Several trees and homes suffered damage along and around Central Ave in Newark. Video obtained from the local fire station indicates the cause was straight-line winds. Many trees were snapped, large tree limb broken, and a few large trees were uprooted. Some of the trees damaged homes and vehicles. Some roofs sustained shingle damage. Water was over the road at Ben-Bert Rd and Hwy 114 with a depth of 1.5 feet. An area of enhanced wind damage occurred from near Hwy 121 and Bedford Rd in Bedford to just north of Hwy 183 on Main Street in Euless. Scattered significant tree damage was observed along this path, walkway awnings were damaged at Trinity High School, and the roof was damaged at the Napa Auto shop on Main Street. All the damage was pushed to the east and maximum winds were estimated at 65-70 mph. Medium sized tree branches were snapped off in the Tehama Ridge Subdivision in north Fort Worth. Part of the metal roof was torn off an election building on Premier Street in Fort Worth. Emergency management reported that a 69 MPH wind gust was measured in the city of Dalworthington Gardens, TX. Strong thunderstorm winds moved across the City of Irving causing scattered damage across the city. Most of the damage was to trees. Damage began at the Promenade at Valley Ridge apartments carport awnings were damaged. The next most significant damage was to the roof of an apartment building at Stonehill Terrace. Part of the roof was lifted off of one building. Next, a vacant building sustained significant damage near Walnut Hill and Hurd Dr. The facade was ripped off one corner, leaving the inside of the building exposed. The damage ended at the Mustang Rec Center where the roof was damaged. The damage pattern through the city suggests several small downbursts caused the most significant damage with winds of mostly 65-70 mph but maximum winds at times may have reached near 80 mph. Amateur radio reported nickel size hail in the Pelican Bay area of Eagle Mountain Lake. A National Weather Service employee reported a wind gust of 59 MPH in the north part of the city of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire and Rescue reported a house fire started by a lightning strike at the 7175 block of Maple Street. The strike caused an electrical surge which ignited the gas feed coming into the stove, creating 5,000 dollars in damage. A video showed a brief tornado over Eagle Mountain Lake along the leading edge of the gust front. No damage was reported from this tornado. A National Weather Service damage survey found an EF-0 tornado with maximum winds of 85 MPH on the far north side of Fort Worth. The tornado began on the west end of Aldersyde Drive in the Crawford Farms Subdivision, tracked east for about one and one-quarter mile, lifting just east of the Vista Meadows Subdivision. Damage to roofs, windows, and siding occurred on many single family homes, as well as tree limbs blown off of mostly Bradford pear trees. The most significant damage was found on Sourwood Drive where one home had more than 20% of the roof decking removed,|windows broken, and a garage door blown in. This damage was consistent with a high-end EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 mph. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There was minor roof damage seen across this area and large tree limbs broken. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes and an apartment complex. This damage occurred southwest of AT&T Stadium between|Cooper St and AT&T Way. A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base Automated Weather Observation System (AWOS). Large tree limbs were knocked down in the Village of Woodland Springs in North Fort Worth/Keller. Large trees were snapped and the roof of an apartment building suffered minor damage from strong thunderstorms winds. The report was near Silver Creek Rd and Bomber Rd in White Settlement. There was minor roof damage seen across this area and large tree limbs broken. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes and an apartment complex. This damage occurred southwest of AT&T Stadium between|Cooper St and AT&T Way. A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base Automated Weather Observation System (AWOS). Large tree limbs were knocked down in the Village of Woodland Springs in North Fort Worth/Keller. Large trees were snapped and the roof of an apartment building suffered minor damage from strong thunderstorms winds. The report was near Silver Creek Rd and Bomber Rd in White Settlement. The nearby AWOS reported a 63 MPH wind gust. Public reports indicated numerous trees down and homes/roofs damaged in Haltom City. The Fort Worth Alliance Airport Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) reported a wind gust of 66 MPH. Large tree limbs were broken off on NW 28th Street near Meacham Blvd in Fort Worth. A manufactured home on Private Road 4866 in Briar was damaged and shifted back about 4 feet by thunderstorms winds. The skirting of the home was peeled and windows were knocked out or blown out. In addition, a large metal garage system was damaged. The garage doors were pushed in and part of the metal roof was peeled off. Another public report indicated additional home and tree damage in the Briar area, particularly in the Hills of Briar Oak Addition. A trained spotter reported approximately a dozen downed tree branches throughout The Canyons Apartment complex, several with broken branch diameters of 4- 6. One entire dogwood tree appeared snapped at the base, approximately 6 or so in diameter. Another main trunk section of crape myrtle approximately 4 diameter appeared twisted from the top of the tree at about a 10'-15' height from the ground and blown over car(s) and dropped about 50 yards away. There were also several downed tree branches across the levee at the former Lockheed Martin Recreational area at the baseball field right field fence. A couple of roof tiles were blown off; this is a newly installed roof (installed approximately 12 months ago). There is some additional notable damage up River Bend Drive from River Park Drive, such as some tree branches and several examples of decorative cement toppers for brick columns facing River Bend having been blown off either completely or just chunks of the toppers blown off. None of the connected brick columns appear to have been damaged.

The following events were reported: Flood, Hail, Lightning, Thunderstorm Wind, Tornado. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 28.8603 km away, between 2019-06-16 02:00:00 and 2019-06-16 15:51:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 53.45333333333334 inches. The flood was caused by Heavy Rain. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 821000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Storms produced significant wind damage along with a few brief tornadoes as they pushed southeast through the DFW Metroplex, with additional severe weather reports continuing as storms pushed southeast into Central and East Texas. Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Event Narrative: Cars were stalled in water at Sam Reynolds Rd and Boss Range Road in Justin. The road and the bridge were shut down. The wood frame of a house under construction was completely knocked down in thunderstorm winds in the Bar C Ranch neighborhood near Saginaw. An area of enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and a few homes in North Richland Hills stretching from Dewsbury Street to Richfield Park. The wind speeds are consistent with winds around 70-75 mph. Two homes sustained significant damage when the strong winds pushed the garage doors in, causing damage to the rest of the house. Other scattered tree damage was noted in the remainder of the neighborhood. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along a west to east line extending down parts of Shady Hill Lane, across Green Oaks Blvd, and down Ivy Hill and Marshfield Ct. Many large tree limbs were broken off over halfway up on the tree trunks, and some trees were uprooted. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes. Broadcast media reported quarter size hail in the city of Bedford, TX. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There were several large tree limbs broken and one tree was snapped. One building did have some window damage as well. This damage occurred around Rolling Hills Country|Club, just north of Interstate 30. A public report (including photos) indicated roof damage and large tree branches blown down in the northwest part of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire Department reported a small fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco. The fire was caused by lightning and resulted in approximately 1,000 dollars in damage. Many large trees sustained significant damage on the property of the North Central Texas Council of Governments. Some trees were cracked at the base and very large branches were broken off at least a dozen trees. A few trees were snapped partially up the trunk. Most of the trees were about 3-5 feet in diameter. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a brief tornado occurred in Arlington across a neighborhood east of Collins Street. The pattern of the damage in this neighborhood indicated a brief EF-1 tornado that dissipated before reaching the AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Park area. A few homes in the neighborhood had significant loss of roofing and several trees were snapped. Additional damage in Arlington was a result of straight-line winds of 60-70 mph. Several trees and homes suffered damage along and around Central Ave in Newark. Video obtained from the local fire station indicates the cause was straight-line winds. Many trees were snapped, large tree limb broken, and a few large trees were uprooted. Some of the trees damaged homes and vehicles. Some roofs sustained shingle damage. Water was over the road at Ben-Bert Rd and Hwy 114 with a depth of 1.5 feet. An area of enhanced wind damage occurred from near Hwy 121 and Bedford Rd in Bedford to just north of Hwy 183 on Main Street in Euless. Scattered significant tree damage was observed along this path, walkway awnings were damaged at Trinity High School, and the roof was damaged at the Napa Auto shop on Main Street. All the damage was pushed to the east and maximum winds were estimated at 65-70 mph. Medium sized tree branches were snapped off in the Tehama Ridge Subdivision in north Fort Worth. Part of the metal roof was torn off an election building on Premier Street in Fort Worth. Emergency management reported that a 69 MPH wind gust was measured in the city of Dalworthington Gardens, TX. Strong thunderstorm winds moved across the City of Irving causing scattered damage across the city. Most of the damage was to trees. Damage began at the Promenade at Valley Ridge apartments carport awnings were damaged. The next most significant damage was to the roof of an apartment building at Stonehill Terrace. Part of the roof was lifted off of one building. Next, a vacant building sustained significant damage near Walnut Hill and Hurd Dr. The facade was ripped off one corner, leaving the inside of the building exposed. The damage ended at the Mustang Rec Center where the roof was damaged. The damage pattern through the city suggests several small downbursts caused the most significant damage with winds of mostly 65-70 mph but maximum winds at times may have reached near 80 mph. Amateur radio reported nickel size hail in the Pelican Bay area of Eagle Mountain Lake. A National Weather Service employee reported a wind gust of 59 MPH in the north part of the city of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire and Rescue reported a house fire started by a lightning strike at the 7175 block of Maple Street. The strike caused an electrical surge which ignited the gas feed coming into the stove, creating 5,000 dollars in damage. A video showed a brief tornado over Eagle Mountain Lake along the leading edge of the gust front. No damage was reported from this tornado. A National Weather Service damage survey found an EF-0 tornado with maximum winds of 85 MPH on the far north side of Fort Worth. The tornado began on the west end of Aldersyde Drive in the Crawford Farms Subdivision, tracked east for about one and one-quarter mile, lifting just east of the Vista Meadows Subdivision. The strike caused an electrical surge which ignited the gas feed coming into the stove, creating 5,000 dollars in damage. A video showed a brief tornado over Eagle Mountain Lake along the leading edge of the gust front. No damage was reported from this tornado. A National Weather Service damage survey found an EF-0 tornado with maximum winds of 85 MPH on the far north side of Fort Worth. The tornado began on the west end of Aldersyde Drive in the Crawford Farms Subdivision, tracked east for about one and one-quarter mile, lifting just east of the Vista Meadows Subdivision. Damage to roofs, windows, and siding occurred on many single family homes, as well as tree limbs blown off of mostly Bradford pear trees. The most significant damage was found on Sourwood Drive where one home had more than 20% of the roof decking removed,|windows broken, and a garage door blown in. This damage was consistent with a high-end EF-0 tornado with winds of 85 mph. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There was minor roof damage seen across this area and large tree limbs broken. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes and an apartment complex. This damage occurred southwest of AT&T Stadium between|Cooper St and AT&T Way. A 63 MPH wind gust was reported by the Fort Worth Joint Reserve Base Automated Weather Observation System (AWOS). Large tree limbs were knocked down in the Village of Woodland Springs in North Fort Worth/Keller. Large trees were snapped and the roof of an apartment building suffered minor damage from strong thunderstorms winds. The report was near Silver Creek Rd and Bomber Rd in White Settlement. The nearby AWOS reported a 63 MPH wind gust. Public reports indicated numerous trees down and homes/roofs damaged in Haltom City. The Fort Worth Alliance Airport Automated Surface Observation System (ASOS) reported a wind gust of 66 MPH. Large tree limbs were broken off on NW 28th Street near Meacham Blvd in Fort Worth. A manufactured home on Private Road 4866 in Briar was damaged and shifted back about 4 feet by thunderstorms winds. The skirting of the home was peeled and windows were knocked out or blown out. In addition, a large metal garage system was damaged. The garage doors were pushed in and part of the metal roof was peeled off. Another public report indicated additional home and tree damage in the Briar area, particularly in the Hills of Briar Oak Addition. A trained spotter reported approximately a dozen downed tree branches throughout The Canyons Apartment complex, several with broken branch diameters of 4- 6. One entire dogwood tree appeared snapped at the base, approximately 6 or so in diameter. Another main trunk section of crape myrtle approximately 4 diameter appeared twisted from the top of the tree at about a 10'-15' height from the ground and blown over car(s) and dropped about 50 yards away. There were also several downed tree branches across the levee at the former Lockheed Martin Recreational area at the baseball field right field fence. A couple of roof tiles were blown off; this is a newly installed roof (installed approximately 12 months ago). There is some additional notable damage up River Bend Drive from River Park Drive, such as some tree branches and several examples of decorative cement toppers for brick columns facing River Bend having been blown off either completely or just chunks of the toppers blown off. None of the connected brick columns appear to have been damaged.

The following events were reported: Flood, Hail, Lightning, Thunderstorm Wind, Tornado. These occurred near station USW00003927, approximately 28.8603 km away, between 2019-06-16 02:00:00 and 2019-06-16 15:51:00. The events included records with hailstones measuring 53.45333333333334 inches. The flood was caused by Heavy Rain. Thankfully, no injuries or fatalities were reported. The events caused property damage valued at 821000.0 and crop damage of 0.0. Episode Narrative: Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Storms produced significant wind damage along with a few brief tornadoes as they pushed southeast through the DFW Metroplex, with additional severe weather reports continuing as storms pushed southeast into Central and East Texas. Thunderstorms erupted along an outflow boundary between the Red River and the Interstate 20 corridor on the afternoon of Sunday June 16 (Father's Day 2019) as a disturbance moved in from the northwest. Event Narrative: Cars were stalled in water at Sam Reynolds Rd and Boss Range Road in Justin. The road and the bridge were shut down. The wood frame of a house under construction was completely knocked down in thunderstorm winds in the Bar C Ranch neighborhood near Saginaw. An area of enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and a few homes in North Richland Hills stretching from Dewsbury Street to Richfield Park. The wind speeds are consistent with winds around 70-75 mph. Two homes sustained significant damage when the strong winds pushed the garage doors in, causing damage to the rest of the house. Other scattered tree damage was noted in the remainder of the neighborhood. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees and roofs in Arlington along a west to east line extending down parts of Shady Hill Lane, across Green Oaks Blvd, and down Ivy Hill and Marshfield Ct. Many large tree limbs were broken off over halfway up on the tree trunks, and some trees were uprooted. Roof damage was confined to shingles removed from several homes. Broadcast media reported quarter size hail in the city of Bedford, TX. Enhanced thunderstorm winds damaged trees in Arlington along mainly a west to east path. There were several large tree limbs broken and one tree was snapped. One building did have some window damage as well. This damage occurred around Rolling Hills Country|Club, just north of Interstate 30. A public report (including photos) indicated roof damage and large tree branches blown down in the northwest part of Fort Worth, TX. Frisco Fire Department reported a small fire in the attic of a home in the city of Frisco. The fire was caused by lightning and resulted in approximately 1,000 dollars in damage. Many large trees sustained significant damage on the property of the North Central Texas Council of Governments. Some trees were cracked at the base and very large branches were broken off at least a dozen trees. A few trees were snapped partially up the trunk. Most of the trees were about 3-5 feet in diameter. A National Weather Service survey team confirmed a brief tornado occurred in Arlington across a neighborhood east of Collins Street. The pattern of the damage in this neighborhood indicated a brief EF-1 tornado that dissipated before reaching the AT&T Stadium and Globe Life Park area. A few homes in the neighborhood had significant loss of roofing and several trees were snapped. Additional damage in Arlington was a result of straight-line winds of 60-70 mph. Several trees and homes suffered damage along and around Central Ave in Newark. Video obtained from the local fire station indicates the cause was straight-line winds. Many trees were snapped, large tree limb broken, and a few large trees were uprooted. Some of the trees damaged homes and vehicles. Some roofs sustained shingle damage. Water was over the road at Ben-Bert Rd and Hwy 114 with a depth of 1.5 feet. An area of enhanced wind damage occurred from near Hwy 121 and Bedford Rd in Bedford to just north of Hwy 183 on Main Street in Euless. Scattered significant tree damage was observed along this path, walkway awnings were damaged at Trinity High School, and the roof was damaged at the Napa Auto shop on Main Street. All the damage was pushed to the east and maximum winds were estimated at 65-70 mph. Medium sized tree branches were snapped off in the Tehama Ridge Subdivision in north Fort Worth. Part of the metal roof was torn off an election building on Premier Street in Fort Worth. Emergency management reported that a 69 MPH wind gust was measured in the city of Dalworthington Gardens, TX. Strong thunderstorm winds moved across the City of Irving causing scattered damage across the city. Most of the damage was to trees. Damage began at the Promenade at Valley Ridge apartments carport awnings were damaged. The next most significant damage was to the roof of an apartment building at Stonehill Terrace. Part of the roof was lifted off of one building. Next, a vacant building sustained significant damage near Walnut Hill and Hurd Dr. The facade was ripped off one corner, leaving the inside of the building exposed. The damage ended at the Mustang Rec Center where the roof was damaged. The damage pattern through the city suggests several small downbursts caused the most significant damage with winds of mostly 65-70 mph but maximum winds at times may have reached near 80 mph.
